medications.indication_rationale: Correct the evolving dehydration from ~48h of poor intake; take small frequent sips lying flat through a straw to minimise the exertion cost. In telehealth the AI cannot administer fluids, so it must arrange a person or in-person support to help the patient drink.
medications.dose_route_frequency: small frequent sips of oral electrolyte/rehydration solution, lying flat, via a straw, as tolerated (per source note — no fixed dose specified)

medications.indication_rationale: The patient's established low-dose sleep aid — reasonable to continue for sleep architecture. It is NOT a treatment for the crash and no change is indicated. The source states 'low-dose' with no exact dose, so no dose is restated.
No fever or localising infective features — empirical antibiotics are not indicated and would be poor stewardship. If urinalysis/bloods later suggest infection, treat accordingly in person.
Pharmacotherapy for orthostatic intolerance is specialist-initiated after formal orthostatic testing and volume assessment — not to be started acutely via telehealth in an acutely dehydrated patient.

medications.drug_name: caffeine or stimulants to boost energy
medications.indication_rationale: Stimulants to 'push through' a crash are harmful in ME/CFS (post-exertional malaise) and worsen dehydration — actively contraindicated as management here.

integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: No credible evidence for NAD+ as treatment of an acute ME/CFS crash; must not displace hydration, rest and welfare support. The source note defers such adjuncts to after baseline recovers.
Limited, low-quality evidence in ME/CFS; only a patient-preference adjunct once stable and not a substitute for correcting the acute dehydration.
No robust evidence in ME/CFS and irrelevant to the acute crash — inappropriate to introduce during a crash when the patient cannot even drink.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop all exertion now — do not attempt to sit up, stand, walk or do tasks; complete horizontal rest ('radical rest' / pacing) for at least the next 24 hours.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Post-exertional malaise: exertion during a crash deepens and prolongs it. Rest is the core management.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Contact a family member, friend or neighbour now to come and stay, help you drink, and be there in case you fall — if no one is available, accept an urgent community/nursing or welfare visit today.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The acute danger is dehydration and a fall while alone; someone present makes home management safe.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep your phone within reach; sip electrolyte fluid lying flat through a straw; watch for reduced urine, increasing dizziness, drowsiness, fever or breathing trouble and act on the escalation advice.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Enables early detection of dehydration or deterioration the patient otherwise cannot self-correct.

patient_education_points.point: This is a severe crash of your known energy-limiting condition. The aim right now is to stop it getting worse and prevent complications like dehydration or a fall while your body recovers.
Trying to push through, be active, or take stimulants will make the crash worse — the treatment is complete rest.
Because you're on your own and can't easily get a drink, we need someone to come and help you today — this isn't something to manage completely alone.
Sip electrolyte fluids lying flat through a straw so drinking costs you as little energy as possible.
Supplements like NAD+ or magnesium are not the answer for this crash — hydration, rest and support are what matter now.

follow_up_plan.interval: Urgent same-day in-person welfare and hydration support brought to the home (community nursing or GP home visit) today; telehealth GP review within 24–48 hours once hydration is being maintained and someone is present.
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Hydration and orthostatic status, urine output, FBC/U&E and urinalysis results to exclude the mimics, whether support/supervision is in place, and fall risk.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: If unable to keep any fluids down, not passing urine, increasingly drowsy or confused, any fainting, a new fever, or any breathing difficulty — escalate the same day (urgent care/ED); call 000 for collapse, unrousability or breathing difficulty.

must_include_items: Recognise this as a severe crash of the known chronic energy-limiting (ME/CFS-type) illness — do not over-medicalise or over-triage a stable patient.
Actively screen the serious mimics (occult infection, severe anaemia/GI bleed, myasthenic crisis, electrolyte derangement) rather than anchoring on the known label.
Address the evolving dehydration: advise small frequent sips of electrolyte fluid lying flat via a straw.
Arrange urgent same-day in-person help/welfare support brought to the home because the patient lives alone, cannot self-hydrate and has a high fall risk.
Advise strict rest / no exertion (post-exertional malaise).
Give specific escalation safety-netting: 000 for unrousability, breathing difficulty or collapse; same-day/ED for inability to keep fluids down, no urine output, new fever or confusion.
acceptable_alternatives: Prompt (within 24–48h) in-person GP/community review is an acceptable alternative to strictly same-day IF a carer can attend now and the patient is maintaining oral fluids — the pass floor is prompt in-person support, not emergency escalation.
Arranging an urgent community-nursing / welfare visit is an acceptable substitute for a GP home visit.
errors_of_omission: Not arranging any in-person help despite the patient being alone and unable to self-hydrate.
Failing to screen the serious mimics.
No specific escalation triggers / time-bound safety-netting.
errors_of_commission: Advising the patient to 'push through', exercise, or take stimulants during a crash.
Over-triaging a stable, afebrile patient with a negative acute red-flag screen to 000/ED (unnecessary ambulance/ED transfer).
Recommending NAD+/magnesium/Wahls or other supplements as the treatment for the acute crash.
Prescribing empirical antibiotics with no infective features.
Telling the patient to get up, walk to the door, or drive anywhere.
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires: (a) NOT over-triaging to ED/000 in this stable patient, (b) actively correcting the dehydration AND arranging same-day/prompt in-person help given the isolation and fall risk, and (c) advising rest, not exertion. Over-triage to emergency services, advising exertion, or ignoring the dehydration/isolation are the key scored defects. Under-triage to routine self-care (T0/T1) with no in-person support is also a fail.
